problems.title: Find Keys by Value
problems.prompt: A normal dictionary lookup starts with a key and finds its value. Here you will perform the opposite search. Write reverse_lookup(mapping, target), which examines every key-value pair and returns a list of every key whose value equals target. Several keys may share the same value, so the result can contain multiple keys; if nothing matches, return an empty list. Preserve dictionary iteration order and do not modify the input.
problems.signature: def reverse_lookup(mapping: dict[object, object], target: object) -> list[object]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def reverse_lookup(mapping: dict[object, object], target: object) -> list[object]:
    """Return every dictionary key associated with a target value.
    
    Args:
        mapping (dict[object, object]): The dictionary whose key-value pairs will be
                                        searched.
        target (object): The value that dictionary entries must equal.
    
    Returns:
        list[object]: A new list of matching keys in dictionary iteration order.
    """
    # Return every key whose value equals target.
    pass

tests.name: finds multiple matches
tests.code:
data = {'red': 1, 'blue': 2, 'green': 1}
assert reverse_lookup(data, 1) == ['red', 'green']

tests.name: handles one or no matches
does not alter its input
tests.code:
data = {'x': [1], 'y': [2], 'z': [1]}
before = dict(data)
assert reverse_lookup(data, [1]) == ['x', 'z']
assert data == before

problems.title: Two-Dice Experiment
problems.prompt: When two ordinary six-sided dice are rolled, totals near 7 occur more often than totals near 2 or 12. Write dice_totals(rolls, seed=None) to simulate this experiment. Perform exactly rolls trials; each trial generates two independent integers from 1 through 6 and adds them. Return a dictionary containing every total from 2 through 12 and its observed count, including totals that occurred zero times. Use a local random.Random(seed) generator so a seed reproduces the same experiment. Raise ValueError for a negative roll count.
problems.signature: def dice_totals(rolls: int, seed: int | None = None) -> dict[int, int]:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import random

def dice_totals(rolls: int, seed: int | None = None) -> dict[int, int]:
    """Simulate repeated rolls of two six-sided dice.
    
    Args:
        rolls (int): The non-negative number of dice trials to simulate.
        seed (int | None): Optional random seed used to make results reproducible.
    
    Returns:
        dict[int, int]: Counts for every possible total from 2 through 12.
    
    Raises:
        ValueError: If rolls is negative.
    """
    # Count the totals from rolling two six-sided dice.
    pass

tests.name: builds a complete frequency table
tests.code:
result = dice_totals(500, seed=12)
assert set(result) == set(range(2, 13))
assert sum(result.values()) == 500
assert all(isinstance(v, int) and v >= 0 for v in result.values())

tests.name: supports deterministic and empty runs
tests.code:
assert dice_totals(40, seed=9) == dice_totals(40, seed=9)
assert dice_totals(0, seed=1) == {n: 0 for n in range(2, 13)}

tests.name: rejects negative trials

problems.title: Old-School Keypad Encoder
problems.prompt: Older mobile phones entered letters by pressing a number key repeatedly. The keys contain these symbols in order: 1='.,?!:', 2='ABC', 3='DEF', 4='GHI', 5='JKL', 6='MNO', 7='PQRS', 8='TUV', 9='WXYZ', and 0=' ' (space). The first symbol takes one press, the second takes two, and so on. Write keypad_presses(message) to return the complete digit sequence. Treat lowercase and uppercase letters alike, and ignore characters not listed on the keypad.

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def keypad_presses(message: str) -> str:
    """Encode a message as presses on a multi-tap numeric keypad.
    
    Args:
        message (str): The text to encode with the supported keypad symbols.
    
    Returns:
        str: The concatenated sequence of keypad digits.
    """
    # Build a character-to-presses dictionary and encode the message.
    pass

tests.name: encodes a complete message
tests.code: assert keypad_presses('Hello, World!') == '4433555555666110966677755531111'

tests.name: handles either case
tests.code:
assert keypad_presses('abc XYZ') == keypad_presses('ABC xyz')
assert keypad_presses('ABC') == '222222'

tests.name: ignores unsupported characters
tests.code:
assert keypad_presses('A;[B]') == '222'
assert keypad_presses('') == ''

problems.title: Morse Message Encoder
problems.prompt: Morse code represents letters and digits with patterns of dots and dashes. Write to_morse(message) using a dictionary containing the standard International Morse mappings for A-Z and 0-9. Translate each supported character and place one ordinary space between encoded characters. Matching is case-insensitive. Skip punctuation, whitespace, and unsupported characters; skipped characters must not create extra spaces in the returned string.

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def to_morse(message: str) -> str:
    """Encode the supported letters and digits in a message as Morse code.
    
    Args:
        message (str): The text whose supported letters and digits should be encoded.
    
    Returns:
        str: Space-separated Morse symbols for each supported character.
    """
    # Map A-Z and 0-9 to standard Morse code.
    pass

tests.name: skips unsupported characters cleanly
tests.code:
assert to_morse(' A--b ') == '.- -...'
assert to_morse('!?') == ''

problems.title: Canadian Postal Code Guide
problems.prompt: Canadian postal codes alternate letters and digits in the form A1A 1A1. Write describe_postal_code(code) and return (region, address_type). Map the first letter as follows: A Newfoundland and Labrador; B Nova Scotia; C Prince Edward Island; E New Brunswick; G/H/J Quebec; K/L/M/N/P Ontario; R Manitoba; S Saskatchewan; T Alberta; V British Columbia; X Nunavut or Northwest Territories; Y Yukon. The second character is 0 for a rural address and any other digit for urban. Accept either case and an optional space. Raise ValueError for a malformed code or invalid first letter.

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def describe_postal_code(code: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
    """Identify the region and address type represented by a Canadian postal code.
    
    Args:
        code (str): A six-character Canadian postal code, with an optional space.
    
    Returns:
        tuple[str, str]: A pair containing the region name and either 'urban' or 'rural'.
    
    Raises:
        ValueError: If code is malformed or begins with an unsupported letter.
    """
    # Validate the code, then identify its region and address type.
    pass

tests.name: identifies urban and rural codes
tests.code:
assert describe_postal_code('T2N 1N4') == ('Alberta', 'urban')
assert describe_postal_code('X0A1B2') == ('Nunavut or Northwest Territories', 'rural')

tests.name: accepts lowercase and optional spacing
tests.code:
assert describe_postal_code('a1a 1a1') == ('Newfoundland and Labrador', 'urban')
assert describe_postal_code('H0H0H0') == ('Quebec', 'rural')

tests.name: rejects invalid codes

problems.title: Numbers in English
problems.prompt: Amounts on cheques are often written as both digits and words. Write number_to_words(number) to convert any integer from 0 through 999 into lowercase English words. For example, 142 becomes 'one hundred forty two'. Do not include 'and', commas, hyphens, doubled spaces, or trailing spaces. Use dictionaries for ones, teens, and tens instead of a long chain of special cases. Raise ValueError when the argument is outside the range or is not an integer.

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def number_to_words(number: int) -> str:
    """Convert an integer from 0 through 999 to English words.
    
    Args:
        number (int): An integer from 0 through 999.
    
    Returns:
        str: The lowercase English representation of number.
    
    Raises:
        ValueError: If number is not an integer from 0 through 999.
    """
    # Convert an integer from 0 through 999 to English words.
    pass

tests.name: handles values below one hundred
tests.code:
assert number_to_words(0) == 'zero'
assert number_to_words(7) == 'seven'
assert number_to_words(16) == 'sixteen'
assert number_to_words(40) == 'forty'
assert number_to_words(58) == 'fifty eight'

tests.name: handles hundreds
tests.code:
assert number_to_words(100) == 'one hundred'
assert number_to_words(142) == 'one hundred forty two'
assert number_to_words(999) == 'nine hundred ninety nine'

tests.name: rejects unsupported values

problems.title: Count Distinct Characters
problems.prompt: Write unique_character_count(text) to report how many different characters appear in text. Compare characters exactly: uppercase and lowercase letters are different, and spaces and punctuation count when present. Repeating a character any number of times still contributes only one. Solve the problem with dictionary keys or a set rather than repeatedly scanning the entire string.

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def unique_character_count(text: str) -> int:
    """Count the distinct characters in a string.
    
    Args:
        text (str): The case-sensitive string whose characters should be counted.
    
    Returns:
        int: The number of unique, case-sensitive characters in text.
    """
    pass

tests.name: counts distinct characters
tests.code:
assert unique_character_count('Hello, World!') == 10
assert unique_character_count('zzz') == 1

tests.name: compares exact characters
tests.code:
assert unique_character_count('Aa! ') == 4
assert unique_character_count('a a') == 2

tests.name: handles empty text
tests.code: assert unique_character_count('') == 0

problems.title: Word Anagram Check
problems.prompt: Two words are anagrams when they contain exactly the same characters with exactly the same frequencies, possibly in a different order. For example, 'evil' and 'live' each contain one e, i, l, and v. Write are_anagrams(first, second) using character-frequency dictionaries. This word-level version compares characters exactly and is case-sensitive. Identical strings count as anagrams because their character collections match.
problems.signature: def are_anagrams(first: str, second: str) -> bool:

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def are_anagrams(first: str, second: str) -> bool:
    """Determine whether two strings contain identical character frequencies.
    
    Args:
        first (str): The first case-sensitive string to compare.
        second (str): The second case-sensitive string to compare.
    
    Returns:
        bool: True when the strings are exact character anagrams; otherwise False.
    """
    # Compare the frequency of every character.
    pass

tests.name: recognizes anagrams
tests.code:
assert are_anagrams('evil', 'live') is True
assert are_anagrams('silent', 'listen') is True

tests.name: checks repeated letter counts
tests.code:
assert are_anagrams('aab', 'aba') is True
assert are_anagrams('aab', 'abb') is False

tests.name: uses exact characters
tests.code:
assert are_anagrams('Tea', 'eat') is False
assert are_anagrams('', '') is True

Compare the frequency of every remaining letter and return True when they match, including when both phrases contain no letters.

tests.name: recognizes phrase anagrams
tests.code:
assert are_phrase_anagrams('William Shakespeare', 'I am a weakish speller') is True
assert are_phrase_anagrams('Dormitory', 'Dirty room!!') is True

tests.name: ignores nonletters and case
tests.code:
assert are_phrase_anagrams('A decimal point', "I'm a dot in place") is True
assert are_phrase_anagrams('123!', '---') is True

tests.code: assert are_phrase_anagrams('the eyes', 'they see it') is False

problems.title: Word Tile Score
problems.prompt: In a word-tile game, common letters are worth fewer points. Score A,E,I,L,N,O,R,S,T,U as 1 point; D,G as 2; B,C,M,P as 3; F,H,V,W,Y as 4; K as 5; J,X as 8; and Q,Z as 10. Write word_score(text) to total every letter in text. Treat uppercase and lowercase alike and ignore spaces, punctuation, digits, and other unsupported characters. Store the letter values in a dictionary.

starterFiles.content:

                    ignored.

tests.name: scores ordinary words
tests.code:
assert word_score('hello') == 8
assert word_score('quiz') == 22

tests.name: is case insensitive
tests.code: assert word_score('Python') == word_score('PYTHON') == 14

tests.name: ignores unsupported characters
tests.code:
assert word_score('a-b c! 123') == 6
assert word_score('') == 0

problems.title: Build a Bingo Card
problems.prompt: A Bingo card has columns B, I, N, G, and O. Each contains five different numbers from its own range: B uses 1-15, I uses 16-30, N uses 31-45, G uses 46-60, and O uses 61-75. Write create_bingo_card(seed=None) and return a dictionary whose keys appear in BINGO order and whose values are five-number lists. Use a local random.Random(seed) generator so the same seed reproduces the same card. Do not use a free centre square in this version.
problems.signature: def create_bingo_card(seed: int | None = None) -> dict[str, list[int]]:

examples.output: A BINGO dictionary with five valid numbers in each column

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
import random

def create_bingo_card(seed: int | None = None) -> dict[str, list[int]]:
    """Create a reproducible five-column Bingo card.
    
    Args:
        seed (int | None): Optional random seed used to make results reproducible.
    
    Returns:
        dict[str, list[int]]: A BINGO-keyed dictionary containing five valid numbers per
                              column.
    """
    # Sample five distinct numbers for each BINGO column.
    pass

tests.name: builds valid columns
tests.code:
card = create_bingo_card(seed=4)
assert list(card) == list('BINGO')
for i, letter in enumerate('BINGO'):
    values = card[letter]; low = i * 15 + 1
    assert len(values) == len(set(values)) == 5
    assert all(low <= value <= low + 14 for value in values)

tests.name: is reproducible
tests.code: assert create_bingo_card(seed=22) == create_bingo_card(seed=22)

tests.name: returns independent cards
tests.code:
first = create_bingo_card(seed=2); second = create_bingo_card(seed=2)
first['B'][0] = 999
assert second['B'][0] != 999

problems.title: Detect a Bingo Win
problems.prompt: A called number on a Bingo card is represented by replacing it with 0. Write is_winning_card(card), where card is a dictionary containing the five BINGO columns as five-item lists. Return True when there are five zeros in any complete horizontal row, vertical column, or either corner-to-corner diagonal. Return False when no complete line is marked. Inspect the card without modifying it. You may assume the dictionary has the required keys and correctly sized lists.

examples.input: A card whose first horizontal row is all zero

starterFiles.name: main.py
starterFiles.content:
def is_winning_card(card: dict[str, list[int]]) -> bool:
    """Check whether a marked Bingo card contains a winning line.
    
    Args:
        card (dict[str, list[int]]): A Bingo-card dictionary with B, I, N, G, and O columns.
    
    Returns:
        bool: True for a complete horizontal, vertical, or diagonal line of zeros.
    """
    # Check vertical, horizontal, and diagonal lines of zeros.
    pass

tests.name: finds horizontal and vertical wins
finds both diagonals
rejects incomplete lines without mutation
tests.code:
card = {letter: [0, 2, 3, 4, 5] for letter in 'BINGO'}; card['O'][0] = 9
before = {k: list(v) for k, v in card.items()}
assert is_winning_card(card) is False
assert card == before

tests.name: unmarked card does not win
tests.code:
card = {letter: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] for letter in 'BINGO'}
assert is_winning_card(card) is False

problems.title: Bingo Simulation
problems.prompt: Estimate how many calls a single Bingo card needs before it wins. Write simulate_bingo(games=1000, seed=None). For each game, create a fresh valid card, shuffle all 75 numbers, call them one at a time, replace matching card numbers with 0, and stop at the first horizontal, vertical, or diagonal line. Return (minimum_calls, maximum_calls, arithmetic_mean) across all games. Use one local random.Random(seed) stream for the complete simulation so results are reproducible. Raise ValueError unless games is a positive integer.
problems.signature: def simulate_bingo(games: int = 1000, seed: int | None = None) -> tuple[int, int, float]:

tests.name: returns plausible statistics
tests.code:
low, high, average = simulate_bingo(30, seed=8)
assert isinstance(low, int) and isinstance(high, int)
assert 5 <= low <= average <= high <= 75

tests.name: is reproducible
tests.code: assert simulate_bingo(12, seed=3) == simulate_bingo(12, seed=3)

tests.name: rejects invalid game counts
supports one complete game
tests.code:
low, high, average = simulate_bingo(1, seed=11)
assert low == high == average
assert 5 <= low <= 75
